skill.name: debugging
skill.description: A four-phase debugging framework for investigating root causes before implementing fixes. It guides agents through reproduction, change analysis, hypothesis testing, focused implementation, and verification.
skill.summary: Debug software issues with a root-cause-first investigation process.
skill.author: ArcadeAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell execution, weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance patterns. Review found no weak cryptography, network activity, malware intent, or prompt injection, but the community skill uses allowed-tools wildcard access and includes shell command examples. It is publishable with a medium-risk warning for broad tool permissions and command execution context.

medium_findings.title: Unrestricted Tool Permission
medium_findings.description: The skill declares allowed-tools wildcard access. This is not malicious by itself, but a community debugging skill with unrestricted tool access can run broad commands if an agent follows unsafe user input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard permission is directly present in the skill metadata. Risk depends on the host agent and user approval model, so this is elevated but not conclusive malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes bash examples for git history inspection and boundary logging. These commands are normal debugging actions, but they require command execution in the user's workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and relevant to debugging. They are not destructive or networked, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: The cited lines do not contain weak cryptographic algorithms, system reconnaissance, or network reconnaissance. The alerts appear to be keyword or markdown false positives in ordinary debugging guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found documentation text, not cryptographic code or reconnaissance behavior. No related malicious intent was found elsewhere in the file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard Tool Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requests all available tools through the allowed-tools wildcard. Marketplace users should understand that this broad permission increases the impact of unsafe agent behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard is explicit in the file. It is a permission risk, but no malicious use of that permission was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace Shell Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to inspect recent changes and add shell logging during debugging. These are legitimate debugging actions but should run only with trusted commands and scoped inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clearly shell commands. They are low-impact examples, but the pattern can become risky if generalized to untrusted commands.

content.value_statement: Software bugs often get worse when agents guess at fixes too early. This skill enforces a structured investigation, hypothesis, test, and verification workflow.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents through a four-phase debugging process.
Requires reproduction and error review before proposing fixes.
Prompts comparison with similar working code in the same project.
Encourages single-hypothesis testing with one change at a time.
Adds verification steps for new tests, existing tests, and real behavior.
Stops repeated failed fixes and asks whether architecture should change.
limitations: Does not diagnose issues automatically without project evidence.
Requires access to relevant logs, errors, tests, or reproduction steps.
May slow down very small fixes because it requires investigation first.
Includes shell command examples that need normal workspace safety controls.

use_cases.title: Investigate failing tests
use_cases.description: Use the framework to read errors, reproduce failures, isolate a hypothesis, and verify the fix with focused tests.

use_cases.title: Stabilize production incidents
use_cases.description: Guide incident work toward evidence, recent changes, and boundary tracing before making risky emergency changes.

use_cases.title: Review repeated failed fixes
use_cases.description: Use the stop rules to identify when patch attempts suggest a deeper design or architecture problem.

prompt_templates.title: Start a bug investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the debugging skill to investigate this bug. First read the error and reproduction steps, then identify the likely root cause before suggesting any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a failing test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the four-phase debugging process on this failing test. Compare the broken path with similar passing code and propose one minimal hypothesis to test.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a multi-layer issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the debugging skill to trace this issue across the API, service, and data layers. Find the first layer where the data becomes wrong.

prompt_templates.title: Recover after failed fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: We tried several fixes and the bug remains. Use the debugging skill to reassess the evidence, stop guessing, and decide whether the design needs review.

output_examples.input: A test fails after a dependency update.
output_examples.output: The agent summarizes the full error, confirms reproduction, checks recent dependency changes, states one hypothesis, and recommends one minimal verification step.
The agent traces request data through each system layer and identifies where expected values first diverge from actual values.

output_examples.input: Three attempted fixes did not resolve a bug.
output_examples.output: The agent stops further patching, reviews what each attempt proved, and recommends an architecture discussion if coupling appears to be the cause.

best_practices: Provide the full error message, stack trace, and recent change history.
Ask the agent to test one hypothesis at a time.
Verify both the new failing case and the existing test suite after the fix.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for a quick fix without sharing evidence.
Do not bundle unrelated cleanup with the bug fix.
Do not continue patching after repeated failures without reassessing the design.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents debug software by finding root causes before proposing fixes.

faq.question: Does it run code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a workflow and examples, but execution depends on the host agent and user approval settings.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill requests wildcard tool access and includes shell command examples, even though no malicious behavior was found.

faq.question: Can it handle production incidents?
faq.answer: Yes, it can structure investigation during incidents, but humans should approve risky operational changes.

faq.question: Does it replace automated tests?
faq.answer: No. It encourages creating or using tests to confirm the bug and verify the fix.

faq.question: Is it useful after a failed fix?
faq.answer: Yes. It explicitly tells the agent to return to investigation and question the architecture after repeated failures.
